title: Origami paper boat
subtitle: Folding a traditional sailing boat with a hull and two masts
excerpt: The origami paper boat is a traditional beginner fold that produces a boat with a hull and two raised triangular sails in about 15 folds. It floats briefly on still water if made from waxed or coated paper. This tutorial covers the standard fold sequence and the finishing step that opens the hull from a flat ring into a standing boat.
sourceNotes: Traditional origami model, documented in Kunihiko Kasahara and Toshie Takahama, Origami for the Connoisseur (1987, Japan Publications), introductory section. Also widely recorded in Joy of Origami (R. Harbin, 1980, Coronet). Public domain by virtue of age and universality of the fold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold in which a pocket or flap is opened and flattened symmetrically around a central axis, usually by holding the model open and pressing down on the layers from above.

A four-pointed square base formed by folding a square in half in both directions and along both diagonals, then collapsing it into a smaller square with four flaps. The starting point for many origami models.

content.text: The origami boat is one of the oldest documented paper folds in Japan and is still often the first model taught to children. The boat begins as a rectangular sheet (not a square), which is unusual in origami, most models start from a square.

Start with a 15 × 15 cm square of kami. Fold in half to make a rectangle (7.5 × 15 cm), coloured side inside. Crease firmly and keep the rectangle in landscape orientation (long edge horizontal) for the next steps.

Fold the top right corner down to the centre bottom edge of the rectangle, making a diagonal fold on the right side. Repeat for the top left corner. You now have a triangular hat shape with a rectangular flap at the bottom. Fold the bottom flap upward on the front layer only, pressing it flat against the bottom of the triangle. Flip the model over and repeat for the other bottom flap.
Form the square and pull open

Insert both index fingers into the hat shape from the open bottom and pull the two open bottom corners apart horizontally. The hat will rotate 45° and flatten into a small square with the points at the sides. Press the
Fold the points and open the hull

On the small square, fold the top point of the front layer down to the bottom point, then fold the same layer back up, leaving a triangular flap at the bottom. Repeat on the back layer. You now have a shape with two triangular points at the top (the masts) and a flat bottom. Open the hull by pulling the bottom open gently from both sides, while pressing the top flat. The two triangular sails rise as the hull expands. Adjust the base until the boat stands flat.

A standard kami boat lasts about 30 seconds on water before the paper absorbs moisture and collapses. To extend this, rub the completed hull lightly with a beeswax block or a white candle stub, coating all surfaces. The wax slows water absorption significantly. Alternatively, fold from a piece of waxed paper or baking parchment, these float for several minutes.
